data.title: Palantir: This AI Train Is Gaining Speed Quickly Again
data.description:
Palantir: AI Rocket Ship Demonstrates Resilience

I must say I'm enthralled by the incredible recovery of Palantir Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:PLTR), outperforming not just the market, but also its software peers. As a result, what seemed like a massive drawdown From the highs about two months ago met with highly resilient dip buying momentum, despite trading at what seems like an astronomical earnings multiple that some pessimists might contend as unsustainable.

In my previous article on PLTR, I had already noted that the stock fell into a bear market after notching stupendous gains to begin 2025. While President Trump’s Liberation Day tariffs did cause market mayhem that led to a broad decline across tech and growth stocks, PLTR’s bottom last week coupled with this week’s early recovery appears to have retaken much of its March losses, browbeating against the bearish prognosticators, although short-sellers appear few and far between with a short interest ratio of just under 2.5% as of end March 2025.

In other words, I believe PLTR’s market outperformance suggests that the market has confidence that:

  • Palantir seems more insulated against the tariffs-induced shock that has inundated its semiconductor and hardware peers. And since Palantir explicitly doesn’t sell its programs and services to China, we shouldn’t expect significant repercussions from the tit-for-tat trade conflict between Donald Trump and Xi Jinping. Yet, we must also not misconceive that there could be blowback from the trade war with global ramifications on enterprise spending, as it remains a crucial growth vertical for Palantir while seeking to diversify its reliance on DoD contracts to justify its expensive growth valuations.

  • The market also seems assured that the earnings profile for application software vendors like Palantir is generally not as badly impacted as the general market. Earnings estimates for the application software industry have remained resilient in April, as compared to the negative revisions for the S&P 500 (SPX, SP500). Hence, it corroborates my opinion that software stocks should continue to outperform even if the trade war were to worsen. The sustained outperformance of PLTR and its software peers (IGV) over the semiconductor industry (SMH) since June 2024 underpins my belief that the market has renewed its focus on the second-order derivative, as AI software platforms or providers become increasingly valuable in the AI supply chain, as they capitalize on their network effect to drive growth and operating leverage, as seen with the surge in valuations and active user base for OpenAI recently.

  • Palantir’s recent NATO contract win marks pivotal progress and validation of the strength of its underlying tech as the transatlantic steps up its defense spending. In addition, it could also open up more opportunities for Palantir in the EU, as Europe seeks to adopt best-in-class AI tech, although trade tensions may escalate, tempering the region’s enthusiasm to work with American tech partners in the near term.

And if that’s not all, consider that Palantir is the best-in-class AI platform for the defense department, and is expected to feature even more prominently in America’s bid to make sure China doesn’t catch up with its AI leadership, while modernizing the defense infrastructure with budget reallocation toward AI imperatives.

President Trump’s recent executive order to revive America’s shipbuilding industry has already driven a surge in collaborations with AI defense providers, suggesting that the opportunities to benefit from the massive defense budget (which could reach a record $1T) cannot be downplayed. Hence, I believe it indicates that even if we were to meet cyclical headwinds due to a slowdown in enterprise spending in 2026, it might not be detrimental to Palantir's growth cadence if the defense component could pick up the slack.

Palantir: Defense Business Provides Robust Support Amid Turbulence

The silver lining is that government revenue accounts for almost 55% of its revenue base over the past twelve months. While commercial revenue has gained traction as enterprise companies reacted positively to its modular go-to-market approach through its AIP boot camps, it seems like Palantir’s government business remains its bread and butter, notwithstanding its more lumpy contractual volatility.

Given a more dangerous geopolitical climate and the U.S. government’s focus on raising defense spending and also restructuring the allocation to AI, I believe PLTR’s ability to benefit must be emphasized. As I enunciated in my previous article, the superiority, and advantages emanating from its AI platform can be entrenched, given how effective its ontology has proved to its defense partners.

Hence, even if there were to be a deceleration in enterprise spending, I believe it might amount to a pause, but not cancellation, suggesting any delays shouldn’t be construed as perpetual. And, it’s critical to point out that Wall Street remains highly confident in Palantir’s earnings resilience. Hence, the company could be well-poised to continue dominating and gain share against the legacy contractors as they struggle with the restructuring while fending off challenges from other AI companies keen on carving out their market share amid the AI transformation and modernization journey.

Buy Or Sell PLTR Stock?

With that in mind, I believe it shouldn’t have surprised you that PLTR’s bottom in April just above the $65 level attracted dip buyers with conviction about the company’s ability to weather the macroeconomic headwinds while relying on its core defense spending prowess and potential resilient enterprise base as we get through the recessionary calls by macro forecasters and strategists.

PLTR’s price action strongly implies that unless the April lows are rejected, the ongoing recovery isn’t expected to falter, notwithstanding near-term volatility in the broad market. Given PLTR's relatively high beta, investors are encouraged to add progressively on pullbacks and not chase FOMO surges or price spikes to improve their risk/reward profile.

Nevertheless, I must also highlight that with a forward EBITDA multiple of almost 140x, it isn’t for the faint of heart. If a recession were to occur, it could torpedo its valuations multiples as compared to much less expensive peers with a software median of just over 15x. In other words, the weight is on Palantir to substantiate its growth thesis, while providing clarity that its enterprise and government spending can be maintained, notwithstanding the consequences of the trade restructuring between America and its trading partners.

Yet, a sustained bottom also provides the driving force for buying momentum to improve further, as investors reallocate to solid outperformers like PLTR, paving the way to revisit its 2025 highs and augmenting a plausible breakout thesis subsequently.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: Abbott Q1: Flexible Supply Chain And Strong Growth In Medical Devices
data.description:
I assigned a "Strong Buy" rating to Abbott Laboratories (NYSE:ABT) in October 2024, highlighting its strong growth in medical device and pharma markets. Since then, the stock price has significantly outperformed the overall market index. I believe its global supply chain and leading positions in the medical device and pharma market could support the company in weathering the tariff and economic uncertainties. I reiterate a "Strong Buy" rating with a fair value of $160 per share.

Abbott released its Q1 result on April 16th, reporting 6.9% organic revenue growth and 8.3% growth excluding the impact of COVID testing, as shown in the table below. The strong growth during the quarter was driven by strong growth in medical device and pharma businesses, growing by 12.6% and 7.8%, respectively.

I think the first question investors should ask is how tariffs impact Abbott’s growth in the near term. During the earnings call, the management noted that the tariff impact could cost the company a few hundred million dollars in 2025, starting from Q3 this year, as there are several months of inventories. I believe Abbott is well positioned in the tariff war, as they have very diversified manufacturing facilities globally, and local production is conducted in most counties. Among the total of 90 manufacturing sites, the company is optimizing these supply chains to minimize the tariff impact. Compared to smaller companies, I believe Abbott has more flexibility navigating the tariff storm.

I think another highlight from the quarter was the strong new product pipelines. During the call, the management indicated that they have around 25 new products in the pipeline over the next three years, which could provide robust support for the company’s future growth. For example, in March 2025, Abbott received a CE Mark in Europe for the Vol PFA System to treat patients battling atrial fibrillation (AFib), with a plan to roll out the product launch across Europe this year.

In addition, in March 2025, Abbott announced the two-year data from the TRILUMINATE clinical trial data, showing substantial improvements in the severity of tricuspid regurgitation treatment after two years. I believe Abbott’s structured heart business will continue to gain market share and deliver strong growth in the coming years.

Lastly, Abbott has done a great job of managing their costs and expanding their margins, delivering 140bps gross margin expansion and 130bps operating margin improvement during the quarter. As a result, their profits before tax increased by 12.9% year-over-year.

Abbott is guiding for 7.5% - 8.5% organic revenue growth with 23.5% - 24.0% of operating margin for FY25. I am impressed by their double-digit EPS growth guidance for FY25, considering the weak economy and tariff uncertainties.

As discussed in my previous article, Medical Device, and Pharma businesses will drive Abbott’s future growth. The company has strong product pipelines with an innovative portfolio in structured hearts, diabetes, vascular, and electrophysiology areas. I believe these new products are more likely to contribute to Abbott’s market share gains and revenue growth. I project its medical device and pharma business will grow by 9% annually, driven by 6% overall market growth and 3% growth from new innovations and market share gains. I assume its diagnostic and nutrition segments will grow by 8% and 5% annually, in line with the historical averages.

Putting together, I calculate the company can deliver 8% organic revenue growth. In addition, I project a 20bps annual margin expansion, driven by 10bps from gross margin improvement from new products, and 10bps from a reduction in SG&A expense thanks to the ongoing restructuring programs. For FY25, I model a one-time $200 million costs caused by tariffs.

With these assumptions, the DCF can be summarized as follows:

The free cash flow from firm (FCFF) calculations can be found below:

The WACC is calculated to be 8.14% assuming: a risk-free rate of 4.2%; beta of 0.7; equity risk premium of 6%; cost of debt of 5%; equity balance of $218 billion; debt of $14.1 billion; tax rate of 16.5%. The terminal growth rate is assumed to be 5% in the model. Discounting all the future FCFF and adjusting cash and debt balances, the fair value is calculated to be $160 per share, as per my estimates.

Downside Risks

The Core Laboratory Diagnostics business only grew by low-single digit during the quarter, driven by weakness in China’s volume-based procurement programs (VBP). The VBP program in China has brought tremendous pressure on pricing for both medical devices and pharmaceutical drugs. Due to the increasing geopolitical risk between China and U.S., I think U.S.-based companies are more likely to face challenges in the domestic market in China, especially in the medical device and pharma markets.

Conclusion

In short, I believe Abbott is well positioned for its medical device and pharma growth, with strong support from its flexible supply chains and new product pipelines. I reiterate a "Strong Buy" rating on Abbott Laboratories with a fair value of $160 per share.

data.title: Pfizer: Tasty 7% Yield
data.description:
The vaccine fears now have Pfizer Inc. (NYSE:PFE) trading down to only $22. The market is now pricing in major fears of lost revenues, with the dividend yield soaring to nearly 8%. My investment thesis is ultra-Bullish on the contrarian play in a leading biopharma company.

Vaccine Fears Mount

The CDC's Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) is set to meet over April 15 and 16 to review U.S. immunization guidelines under HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. The meeting will include presentations on the Covid-19 and RSV vaccines, along with a discussion on the measles outbreak.

The market is naturally jittery regarding vaccine approvals, with RFK Jr. being a notable vaccine skeptic, though he has publicly stated no desire to block a patient from obtaining a vaccine. The news of him recently questioning the efficacy of the Novavax (NVAX) Covid-19 shot that just missed a key deadline for FDA approval casts a large shadow over the industry.

As previously outlined, RFK Jr. testified to Congress he would let the data decide the outcomes of vaccines. His negative views on vaccines have to be verified by data in order to block a vaccine, especially one desired by patients. The Novavax Covid vaccine looking for FDA approval to move beyond emergency use is a key sign of whether RFK Jr. has the data to back up his view of vaccine skepticism.

Pfizer has a strong pipeline outside of vaccines. Padcev, Braftovi, Lorbrena and Elrexfio all target cancer treatments with strong growth profiles, including opportunities to become mega blockbuster drugs.

At the recent TD Cowen conference, CEO Albert Burl outlined drugs with the potential to become mega blockbuster as follows:

Mega blockbuster, it's anything above $2.5 billion, something like that, right? So significantly different than the previous ones. I just mentioned two that we think will become like that, the two ADCs. CDK4, it is something that will also fall into this category if it is successful.

ELREXFIO, from the products that we launched, we believe will fall in this category. This is for multiple myeloma. Actually, ELREXFIO, when I see these three testaments, this is one of the products that we have a very big difference between our estimations and what the Street is forecasting. We are very confident, and we are very happy with how the product is moving.

Pfizer grew 2024 sales outside of Covid drugs by 12%. The biopharma had about $11 billion in Covid related drug sales in 2024 for Paxlovid and Comirnaty, making Pfizer far less reliant on these drug sales and at risk to RFK Jr. pushes to reduce vaccine usage.

The company has a large amount of drugs, looking to reach $2.5+ billion in annual sales. Not to mention, the Seagen deal has long been targeted to reach $10 billion in annual sales after reaching $3.4 billion in 2024.

Soaring Yield

Pfizer has now seen the dividend yield surge to nearly 8%. The stock has fallen to decade lows, while the company actually hiked the dividend by 2.4% to $0.43 per share each quarter.

The company expects to pay out an annual dividend of $1.72 amounting to $9.6 billion annually in cash to shareholders. Pfizer guided to a 2025 EPS of up to $3, an amount that nearly repeats the 2024 levels.

The biopharma now pays a dividend yield approaching 8% in a signal of the fear in the stock. The yield has roughly doubled the levels from the prior decade, when Pfizer regularly had a more normal 4% dividend yield.

The company still generates similar profits, if not higher, than in the last decade. Pfizer generates ~$13 billion in operating cash flows, providing substantial cash flows to cover R&D spending and paying the large dividend.

The biopharma even sold another $3.2 billion worth of Haleon (HLN) shares. Pfizer unloaded a $3.0 billion position in January, providing over $6 billion in cash during Q1.

The stock trades below 8x EPS target for 2025. Pfizer definitely has some risk regrading further reductions in vaccine and Covid related sales, but the biopharma also could see some upside to Covid drug sales.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Pfizer now offers a very compelling dividend yield. The market appears far too bearish on RFK Jr. cutting vaccine sales while the biopharma moves away from a business focused on vaccines.

Investors should use the ongoing weakness to load up on the stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: American Integrity Insurance Starts IPO Rollout For Expansion Plans
data.description:
American Integrity Is Growing Revenue Slowly But Plans Expansion

American Integrity Insurance Group, LLC (AII) has filed to raise $100 million in an IPO of its common stock, according to an SEC S-1 registration statement.

AII provides admitted property & casualty insurance to homeowners located in the state of Florida.

The firm is growing topline revenue slowly in the most recent two calendar year periods, but management seeks to grow the topline and increase profitability through expansion and optimization efforts.

I'll provide an update when we get more information about pricing and valuation assumptions from management.

What Does American Integrity Do?

Tampa, Florida-based American Integrity Insurance Group, Inc. was founded in 2006 to primarily serve the Florida residential property insurance market.

Management says that

"according to the FLOIR [Florida Office of Insurance Regulation], we are the seventh largest writer of residential property insurance in Florida based on DPW [Direct Premiums Written] for policies in-force as of December 31, 2024."

Management is headed by founder and CEO Mr. Robert Ritchie, who has been with the firm since its inception in 2006 and was previously Vice President for American Modern Insurance Group (Munich Re) and Senior Vice President for GE Insurance Solutions.

The company’s primary offerings include the following:

  • Voluntary Market - 79% of revenue.

  • Citizens policy assumption - 21% of revenue.

As of December 31, 2024, American Integrity has booked a fair market value investment of approximately $10.3 million from investors, including Sowell Investments Holding Co., LLC.

The company currently obtains a majority (about 80%) of its revenue by selling residential insurance policies directly to homeowners (Voluntary Market) and the remaining 20% from selectively assuming insurance obligations from the state-owned Citizens insurance entity.

Prior to 2024, for many years AII did not assume policies from Citizens, but due to reforms in various aspects of Citizens' operation, the company has restarted that line of business.

General & Administrative costs as a function of topline revenue have trended lower as revenues have risen, as the figures below indicate:

General & Administrative

Expenses vs. Revenue

Period

Percentage

Year Ended Dec. 31, 2024

15.1%

Year Ended Dec. 31, 2023

17.0%

(Source - SEC).

The General & Administrative efficiency multiple, i.e., how much new revenue is generated by each dollar of General & Administrative expense, 0.1x in the most recent reporting period. (Source - SEC).

The company has significant geographic concentration risks with the vast majority of its premiums written in Florida but is seeking to expand into Georgia and South Carolina.

AII has limited related party transactions, as detailed here, but the president of the company is Jon Ritchie, the son of the founder and CEO Robert Ritchie.

Market Overview For American Integrity

According to a 2023 market research report [PDF] by National Association of Insurance Commissioners, the US market for homeowners multiple peril insurance premiums was $133.77 billion in 2022.

With Florida then accounting for 10.8% of total US homeowners' premiums, Florida's market was approximately $14.45 billion in size.

The report noted that the "total direct premium written in-state has increased every year since 2013."

In 2022, the overall insurance industry ranked the largest among all financial institution industries in Florida, with 50.51% of the total gross domestic product share, as the chart shows here:

Increasing risks from more intense storms and hurricanes will likely put upward pressure on insurance premiums and high-risk area coverage exclusions.

Various competitive or other industry participants include the following:

  • Tower Hill Insurance Group.

  • Florida Peninsula Insurance Company.

  • Frontline Insurance Unlimited Company.

  • Southern Oak Insurance Company.

  • The Progressive Corporation (PGR).

  • State Farm.

  • Chubb Limited (CB).

  • Amica.

  • Liberty Mutual.

Also, in 2022, Florida had a total of 2,132 domestic and licensed foreign insurers.

More private insurers are expected to enter the Florida market as a result of recent legislative reforms that reduce excessive litigation.

I’ve prepared a thumbnail SWOT analysis for American Integrity:

Strengths

  • Large presence in Florida, track record of profitability, strong reinsurance program, vertically integrated underwriting, claims and risk management model.

Weaknesses

  • Geographic concentration risk, exposure to hurricane and severe weather catastrophe risks, limited diversification of service lines.

Opportunities

  • Geographic expansion to South Carolina and Georgia, improving Florida legislative developments, increasing distribution partnerships, reduction of quota share reinsurance to keep more profit.

Threats

  • Climate change is increasing catastrophic risks, regulatory risks, litigation trends and fraud risks.

American Integrity’s Recent Financial Results

The firm’s recent financial performance is summarized as shown below:

  • Slowly growing topline revenue.

  • Increasing income before income taxes.

  • Sharply growing cash flow from operations.

Below are major financial results from the firm’s current registration statement:

Total Revenue

Period

Total Revenue

% Variance vs. Prior

Year Ended Dec. 31, 2024

$204,354,000

1.7%

Year Ended Dec. 31, 2023

$200,871,000

Income (Loss) Before Income Taxes

Period

Income (Loss) Before Income Taxes

Year Ended Dec. 31, 2024

$51,039,000

Year Ended Dec. 31, 2023

$44,755,000

Comprehensive Income (Loss)

Period

Comprehensive Income (Loss)

Comprehensive Margin

Year Ended Dec. 31, 2024

$40,450,000

19.8%

Year Ended Dec. 31, 2023

$40,017,000

19.9%

Cash Flow From Operations

Period

Cash Flow From Operations

Year Ended Dec. 31, 2024

$148,909,000

Year Ended Dec. 31, 2023

$64,438,000

(Glossary Of Terms)

(Source - SEC)

As of December 31, 2024, American Integrity had $179.3 million in cash and $1.0 billion in total liabilities.

Of the $1 billion in total liabilities, approximately $475 million were for accrued unpaid losses and loss adjustment expenses.

The company has generated free cash flow for the twelve months ended December 31, 2024, of $147.6 million.

IPO Details For American Integrity

American Integrity intends to raise $100 million in gross proceeds from an IPO of its common stock, although the final amount may differ.

No existing shareholders have shown a desire to purchase shares of the IPO, and it appears there is interest in selling shareholder[s] in selling shares into the IPO.

The company, directors, officers and an as-yet-undetermined characterization of Existing Owners will be subject to the standard 180-day shareholder sale lock-up period from the effective date of the prospectus.

Management disclosed it intends to apply the net proceeds from the IPO as follows:

We intend to use the net proceeds from this offering for general corporate purposes, which may include contributing capital to AIICFL [American Integrity Insurance Company of Florida, Inc.] to support growth.

We will not receive any proceeds from the sales of Common Stock by the Selling Stockholders in this offering. We will, however, bear the costs associated with the sale of shares of Common Stock by the Selling Stockholders, other than underwriting discounts and commissions.

(Source - SEC).

The company's online roadshow is not yet available.

Regarding outstanding legal proceedings, management said the firm is not subject to any legal proceedings that would have a material adverse effect on its financial condition or results of operations.

The listed bookrunners of the IPO are Keefe, Bruyette & Woods, Piper Sandler, William Blair, Citizens Capital Markets, Raymond James, and Oppenheimer & Co.

American Integrity Is Growing Slowly With Plans To Expand

AII is seeking public capital market investment to fund its general growth and expansion initiatives.

The firm’s financials have shown slowly growing topline revenue, higher income before income taxes and much higher cash flow from operations.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended December 31, 2024, was $147.6 million.

The company's growth strategy is to expand into the states of Georgia and South Carolina, launch new product offerings and reduce its reinsurance dependency to improve profitability, so will likely use the IPO proceeds for this expansion activity.

General & Administrative expenses as a percentage of total revenue have fallen as revenue has grown; its General & Administrative efficiency multiple was only 0.1x in the most recent calendar year.

The firm currently intends to pay no dividends and will likely retain any future earnings for its growth, investment, and working capital requirements.

AII did not spend on CapEx in 2024.

The market opportunity for selling residential insurance in Florida and other states in the U.S. is large but competitive and subject to the potential for increasing weather-related risks.

The company will have risks as a public entity, including its ‘emerging growth company’ status, which will allow management to disclose less information to shareholders.

Many of these types of company stocks have performed poorly post-IPO, although U.S.-based specialty insurance providers have performed well.

When we learn more details about the IPO’s pricing and valuation assumptions, I’ll provide a final opinion.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced.

data.title: Inflation Expectations Suggest A Further Major Fall In S&P 500
data.description:
At the end of 2024, I thought that there was a good chance that the market (NYSEARCA:SPY) would be up in 2025. With the new tariffs announced, some of which have been implemented, I think that the 30% chance of a bear market that I saw then, is now much closer to 90%. Read on to find out how I reached this conclusion.

Long-term inflation expectations have risen

The most recent University of Michigan consumer survey of inflation expectations shows that inflation in the year ahead continues to rise, with an expected value of 6.7%, in line with the level seen in 2022. Interestingly, they break down the survey results by political party association and find that independents are better at predicting inflation than partisans, irrespective of which party holds the White House.

More worryingly, long-term inflation expectations are now at 4.4% (see graph below), which was not seen in 2022. Passing over 2022 because long-term inflation expectations didn’t change then, the last time long-term inflation expectations were above 4% were in 1990–1991 and 1979–1985 (1979 was the first year of the survey). In both cases, inflation only dropped because of a recession caused by interest rates being raised to fight inflation.

I suspect that the survey still underestimates the likely path of inflation, as there is still hope that there will be exemptions granted from tariffs, similar to the way that country-specific tariffs were paused for negotiations to occur. However, even in the absence of further tariffs or the reimposition of country-specific tariffs, I suspect the current 10% global tariff level combined with steel, car tariffs, and tariffs on China alone are likely to be sufficient to achieve more than a 7% inflation level. The inflation level is likely to be even higher if the Mexico and Canada tariffs remain, which I believe Trump will hold to use as leverage for a USMCA renegotiation.

Inflation and market valuations are linked

I’m a data person, so I like to look at historical patterns as part of my forecasting process. The chart below from Goldman Sachs Research ("Bear Market Anatomy – the path and shape of the bear market") is worrying when you use it to estimate the likely forward P/E ratio with long-term inflation at 4.4%. Using the P/E from the 1990–1991 and 1979–1985 periods (see above), gives an expected forward P/E of 10–15x for the market with high inflation.

When these multiples are applied to the current market, the chart below (again from Goldman Sachs Research, as of April 4, 2025) shows that the market could fall another 20–30% if companies increase their earnings in the next 12 months by 3% and could fall a further 40% if earnings decline by 11% as companies try to absorb some of the impacts of tariffs. This gives a target for the bottom of the market for the S&P 500 of as low as 3,100. It may also fall further than this if earnings decline by more than 11%, depending on the future path of tariffs and budget deficit cuts.

You could argue that there have been large changes in the market and the economy since the 1980s and 1990s. And I agree, but I wanted to get a sense of how higher long-term inflation expectations would affect the market, which hasn’t happened since then. I also checked the reasonableness of the P/E multiples against more recent downturns and found a forward P/E of 14x was also reached during 2018 at the bottom of the market and that the forward P/E in 2009 was below 10x. I'm interested to know if anyone reading this thinks that the market could go as low as a forward P/E of 10x in the next year or so.

Where do you invest if it's going to get this bad?

There are a number of different answers to this, but a passive investment in the S&P 500 in SPY should only be taken if you expect to stay invested as the market drops and intend to wait for the market to rebound. As you can see in the graph below (note this is MSCI AC World rather than S&P 500, from Goldman Sachs Research), if you are prepared to wait up to four years, then the market will eventually turn upward again. You’ll have to decide for yourself whether you think the current market downturn is likely to be short-lived, as tariffs are a one-off shock like Covid in 2020, or whether the downturn will persist for longer, as occurred in 2000–2002.

The easiest way to avoid losing money is to move to 100% cash, but this is not ideal when you are expecting inflation and interest rates to increase by over 5%. One way to avoid the impact of inflation is moving into currencies that are less likely to experience inflation, such as the euro. Stock markets in other countries are also likely to experience a downturn (see table below, percentages are in local currency not USD), but changes in the strength of these currencies versus the US dollar may reduce the size of the losses.

Another option is investing in inflation-protected bonds such as VTIP, TIP, SCHP, or STIP. Gold is also an option, either through physical gold via GLD or IAU, or gold mining companies such as AEM. The standard defensive sectors such as utilities, insurance, and defense are also an option, but you would need to pick carefully. Healthcare is generally included as a defensive sector, but the uncertain state of future tariffs on healthcare, uncertainty about actions of the Department of Health and Human Services, and uncertainty about possible cuts to Medicare or Medicaid in the federal budget mean that I can’t recommend investments at this time.

The final option is to hold cash to go bargain hunting, weighing up the extent to which a company will be impacted by tariffs and where its valuation level is sitting at relative to historical averages. My only recent long-term purchase was GOOG at $150, as I believe its globally diversified revenue, revenue largely not reliant on goods subject to tariffs (noting the potential for retaliation against big tech by other countries), investments for the future in areas such as self-driving cars and most of all P/E of 17x at the time justified this purchase.

Risks to my outlook

My current outlook assumes that President Trump intends to keep the baseline 10% global tariffs as a non-negotiable position and that the Senate will not act to remove the ability to apply tariffs from the President. This is consistent with their current stated positions, but it is possible that their positions may change over time as deals are done with other countries and/or the market continues to drop. My expectation is that the market will need to drop significantly again before any actions are taken to remove the baseline tariffs.

It also assumes that the level of inflation expected over the next twelve months is an accurate prediction and that then leads to a continued expectation of future inflation. History suggests that temporarily high inflation will lead to wage increases for employees (where companies remain in business), which then leads to further inflation unless credible efforts are made to reduce the expectation of longer-term inflation. I expect to see a war of words between the Federal Reserve and the government about the right level of interest rates to address the impact of tariffs, which may progress to attempts to remove some of the Board of Governors by the President or Senate.

Summary

My outlook for the market is much more bearish than it was three months ago, and I believe that there is now a 90% chance of a bear market in 2025. Now is the time to consider where you wish your portfolio to be invested (if you haven’t already moved). SPY is only a good option if you are prepared to hold your investment as its value continues to drop, consider investments in inflation-protected bonds, markets outside of the USA, defensive sectors, or bargain hunting.

data.title: Investing In Uncertain Times
data.description:
Some worthwhile macro observations in this discussion

DoubleLine Deputy CIO Jeffrey Sherman moderates a discussion on April 3, the day after President Donald Trump’s reciprocal tariff announcement, between DoubleLine CEO-CIO Jeffrey Gundlach and Quill Intelligence CEO and Chief Strategist Danielle DiMartino Booth on the current state of the market and economic outlook. The discussion was held as part of a DoubleLine Client Event in Dallas on April 3, 2025.

A few charts along for the ride

Household liquidity is at its lowest in more than five years. Savings are mostly tied up in real estate and a record concentration in the stock market.

In the recent decline, the S&P 500 forward price-to-earnings multiple has fallen to about 19x earnings from 24.4x last September. The median P/E at bear market bottoms since 1982 has been 13x. To restore the 43-year median and correct for the extreme overvaluations of the previous 20 years, either prices will need to fall significantly more, or earnings will need to leap from here.

At the moment, falling revenue projections and rising input costs are pushing earnings expectations in the opposite direction.

So, further price declines are needed to improve the investment return outlook. Since World War II, the average bear market decline for stock prices has been 35%, ranging from -25 to -55%. This is not happy news for the always-be-long crowd, but it is promising for those with patience and a risk-adjusted value discipline.

Happy Easter.

Disclosure: No positions

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Prologis: Strong Buy, Tariffs Are Likely Temporary
data.description:
Downgrades have put the top REIT into strong buy territory

Anyone who follows REITs and has analyzed the REIT sector universe of index funds closely knows that Prologis, Inc. (PLD), is usually the top holding in most cap-weighted ETFs over the past couple of years. The cell tower REITs normally rank right behind them, followed by storage, data center, and brick-and-mortar retail. Market sentiment driving these market caps says that these are the most relevant and investable real estate sub-sectors.

I agree that while physical retail has a few looming questions [mainly due to e-commerce and logistics like Prologis], given a long enough time frame, all of these categories should see consistent growth following economic trajectories. Let's take a look at some of the biggest REITs by market cap:

We can see the market cap of Prologis jockeying with Welltower Inc. (WELL) and American Tower Corporation (AMT) and for the time being, has been surpassed/ After a two-year run which saw Prologis as the top dog REIT of the pack, the company is now seeing souring sentiment with the "R" word being thrown around.

How far has this fallen?

-43% off the all-time high is up there with the biggest REIT thrashings and amazingly enough, it still remains a top 3 market cap company in the REIT sector. That tells you that the entire sector has been horrible, especially since the rate hikes in 2022. For those with a long runway that like to bottom feed, REITs in my opinion are some of the best fishing grounds for mean reversion plays. I don't know how long it will take for that mean to revert, but I'm adding patiently to my favorite REITs.

Prologis right now is by far my favorite.

I digress that there is some reason to be worried, analysts and Wall St. have all too fond of memories of what happened to Prologis in the last real deep recession of 2008-2011:

A big draw-down saw Prologis trading below $10 a share for a period in 2009, let's reminisce:

Shares of ProLogis fell $2.40, or almost 35 percent, to close at $4.47 on the New York Stock Exchange. Smaller rival AMB Property dropped 30 percent, or $5.70, to $13.25.

Since the beginning of the year, ProLogis shares have lost 93 percent of their value.

"Considering the stock's recent performance, these look like appropriate actions at first glance," Michael Mueller, J.P. Morgan analyst, said in a research note. "Equally important, the company will still need to address liquidity concerns in terms of how it will deal with upcoming maturities in these challenging times, and any implications for its investment management business over the long-term."

The CEO departed, and the dividend was cut hard in 2009. Logistics centers were deemed to be overbuilt and there was too much vacancy in a terrible recession. People couldn't pay their mortgages, and forget about having Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) boxes dropped off at the doorstep daily [like we do now].

How much has e-commerce grown since 2009?

E-commerce has grown a lot. The period just prior to and including Covid lockdowns saw e-commerce absolutely explode. More tepid growth rates are expected in the future years to come, but logistics centers, like the ones Prologis runs are becoming ever more important and one day may replace most physical retail completely in my opinion.

Smartphones and digital payment systems have unlocked nearly all friction that may have existed between the consumer and the warehouse. Having the Amazon app open is now more dangerous than going to Costco for your wallet.

The dividend is the best in a decade

We can see that over the last decade, Prologis has commanded a pretty hefty premium for its growth rates and investors paid up for a 2-3% dividend consistently. We now see a spike up to 4+%, the highest since 2016. I always hunt for these decade-high dividend spikes and ask myself about sustainability. For now, it looks great. With a trailing AFFO yield of 4.76% and a current dividend of 4.11%, that's a payout of AFFO of 86.3%. This is well within the realm of logic for REITs and their payouts.

AFFO yield vs the 10-Year risk-free rate

Any good investor when doing a basic analysis should look at the risk-free rate compared to the earnings yield of the investment they are contemplating. Your risk-free rate is static. Your at-risk investment should grow its earnings yield. If you do not anticipate future growth and only deterioration, then certainly stay away. If you anticipate future growth and a higher than risk-free rate yield that grows and compounds, then start your analysis there:

  • 4/15/2025 10-Year yield: 4.37%.
  • 4/15/2025 Prologis AFFO yield: 4.76%.
  • Prologis 5-Year FFO CAGR: 10.93%.

A caveat to the growth rates

Looking at the previous projections for e-commerce growth, the next few years expect high single-digit growth rates. This is probably a realistic assumption as well for logistics REITs involved in e-commerce. Lowering our growth rate assumptions to 7% annual CAGR, a dividend growing at that rate for the next decade with a starting point of 4.11%, gets you to an 8.08% yield on cost a decade later.

If, however, we are being too conservative in our assumptions and Prologis can achieve that same 10.93% CAGR over the next decade then the yield on cost a decade later ends up being11.59%. Either way, barring a dividend cut, I think investors would do well here versus a fixed-income alternative over the next decade.

Double downgrades and looming tariffs

Scotia Bank and others have been negatively downgrading the outlook for Prologis. Bigger tariffs on Chinese goods make the assumption that all e-commerce will see a volume decrease as a result of less-needed logistics warehousing. The actual revenue number in commerce may not go down, but the volume of stuff might. I am of the opinion that tariffs will not be long-lasting and are a bargaining tool to get the opposition to the table for Trump.

That's just my opinion, and I'm sticking to it. I believe after this trajectory fails, the plan will be to switch to "growing your way out" of a crisis by 2026 rather than tax or tariff your way out.

Earnings April 16th

Earnings are out, and everything looks stable for now:

Prologis, Inc. (PLD) delivered stronger-than-expected Q1 earnings and revenue on Wednesday and reaffirmed its 2025 earnings guidance, but lowered expectations for dispositions and development starts as its customers become wary amid policy uncertainty.

The industrial REIT reaffirmed its guidance for 2025 core FFO per share at $5.65-$5.81, compared with the average analyst estimate of $5.75.

Other positives Prologis has going for it

Prologis has centers that service all levels of the e-commerce supply chain. This includes return centers that are possibly one of the most important aspects of e-commerce.

Prologis is the biggest logistics retail REIT and is internationally diverse. Yes, the company has more than half of its operations in the US, but 491 million square feet is on ex-US soil. That's around 37% of the portfolio that is likely not subject to US tariff effects.

Just like Realty Income Corporation (O) presentations, Prologis reminds you that they have an A3/A credit rating by S&P and Moody's. With a weighted average interest rate of 3.2% and 9 years to maturity on most debt, that is lower than most US home mortgage re-financiers got during COVID-era ZIRP rates.

When we think of logistics, we just think of e-commerce sales. However, not only is everyone in e-commerce now, but they also need to store inventory for their brick-and-mortar locations. The customer TAM for logistics is as infinite as the choices of businesses that seek to sell physical goods into the worldwide economy.

Risks and summary

I believe the risk here is mostly in sentiment around international e-commerce. There could be blanket downgrades just based on tariff talk alone without any thoughtful analysis by Wall St. I see every dip and downgrade in e-commerce names and adjacent stocks like Prologis to be buying opportunities. I believe the online shopping genie is out of the bottle never to get stuffed back in, just like AI growth.

I can't imagine this doing well in the near term with tariff talks abound, but in the long term, I think buying at these levels with a decade-high dividend makes sense. Buy.

data.title: U.K. Inflation Update March 2025: GBP/USD Market Analysis And BoE Rate Cut Predictions
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

The Office for National Statistics (ONS) released UK inflation data for March this morning. The data revealed that the UK's annual inflation rate dropped to 2.6% in March 2025, down from 2.8% in February and below the expected 2.7%. The biggest price decreases came from recreation and culture, particularly games, toys, and hobbies (-4.2%) and data processing equipment (-5.1%). Transport also played a role, with motor fuel prices falling by 5.3%.

Price increases slowed for restaurants and hotels (3%, the lowest since July 2021), housing and utilities (1.8%), and food and non-alcoholic drinks (3%). On the other hand, clothing and footwear prices rose by 1.1%, reflecting typical increases as spring fashions hit stores.

Monthly inflation rose by 0.3%, slightly less than the previous month's rise and below predictions of 0.4%. Core inflation, which excludes volatile items, eased to 3.4% from 3.5%.

Inflation moving forward and implications for the Bank of England (BoE)

Following the inflation data, markets are pricing in around an 85% probability of a rate cut from the BoE at the Central Banks May meeting.

The bigger question for consumers at least is whether this is as good as it is going to get? There is a school of thought among analysts that inflation has likely bottomed and that markets and consumers need to prepare for higher inflation moving forward.

The reason is largely to do with energy prices, as ING THINK put it well, stating energy bills have mostly helped lower inflation due to the big drop in natural gas prices after the 2022 spike. However, starting in April, energy bills will add 0.8 percentage points more to the annual CPI than they did in March. Water bills have also gone up significantly this month.

With this in mind, ING forecasts put April’s CPI figure at 3.2%, rising to 3.5% or maybe even a tad higher towards the end of the third quarter.

Personally, I do not see such a huge jump in April largely on the back of global uncertainty which, I think, is already impacting demand and spending habits. This could lead to consumers spending less and prioritizing savings due to an uncertain economic outlook, and thus help keep Global inflation in check.

Of course, this will also depend on how tariff negotiations shake out, as this could in theory also lead to an increase in inflation, thus negating my assessment of lower demand and steady inflation.

Services inflation also still remains uncomfortably high, but is on target to reach the BoEs forecast figure.

All in all, an interesting period ahead for the UK economy, something the rest of the world is likely to grapple with as well for the majority of 2025.

Technical Analysis - GBP/USD

Looking at GBP/USD from a technical standpoint, the rally to the upside has broken above the resistance level at 1.3261. However, GBP/USD needs to record a daily close above the 1.3261 for further gains to materialize.

The 14-period RSI is also approaching overbought territory which could hinder further upside.

A modest recovery by the US Dollar is what kept GBP/USD from advancing yesterday and early session weakness today is allowing cable to move higher.

I stand by my analysis yesterday, this move is largely being driven by the weaker US dollar rather than GBP strength.

GBP/USD Daily Chart, April 16, 2025

Support

  • 1.3261
  • 1.3100
  • 1.3000

Resistance

  • 1.3322
  • 1.3415
  • 1.3500

Original Post

data.title: China's Posts Solid Growth, But More Support Likely Needed To Meet 5% Target
data.description:
By Lynn Song

China's first-quarter GDP showed a strong start to the year

China's first-quarter growth registered a stronger-than-expected 5.4% year on year, beating our 5.3% forecast and market expectations for 5.2% growth. The economy is off to a much-needed strong start in 2025, as second-quarter growth will likely take hits from the sharp escalation of President Trump’s trade war.

Once again, the manufacturing sector supported secondary industry growth of 5.9% YoY, the highest level since the first quarter of 2024. Moving forward, the trade-war impact on manufacturing will be watched closely to see if China’s overcapacity problem gets worse. Despite strong first-quarter manufacturing activity, we saw the industrial capacity utilisation rate slumped from 76.2% to 74.1%.

The secondary industry has now outpaced the tertiary industry growth for five straight quarters. With manufacturing likely to take a hit from tariffs, China’s ability to maintain solid growth moving forward could depend on the tertiary industry helping to pick up the slack.

This year's policy will be focused on supporting domestic demand. One important aspect will be building out the services sector, including childcare and elderly care and so-called "life services" like catering, domestic services, healthcare and tourism. We will see if these efforts bear fruit in the coming quarters.

China 1Q25 GDP showed a strong start to the year

China's property prices edge closer toward a trough

China's 70-city property price data saw a smaller sequential decline in both new home and used home prices in March, with prices down -0.08% and -0.23% month on month, respectively.

Despite continued declines, the data once again offers some reasons for optimism. For example, 29 cities saw new home prices stable or increasing, while 14 saw used home prices stable or rising.

In the new home market, 18 cities eked out gains of under 1% in the first quarter, while five saw prices rise over 1%. On the flip side, 39 cities saw price declines of under 1%, while nine saw a price drop of over 1%. The used-home market showed a slightly weaker picture, with four cities seeing price gains of under 1%, one with prices rising more than 1%, 39 with price drops under 1%, and 26 with prices dropping more than 1%. No city saw prices drop more than 2%.

This data suggests that property prices are in the process of establishing a trough.

Amid China's attempt to increase confidence and boost domestic demand, we believe that stabilising the property market remains a paramount priority. It’s difficult to imagine consumers spending confidently as long as their biggest asset continues to lose value every month. March data should be seen as another small step in the right direction.

More cities are starting to see prices edge up

Strong beat in retail sales showed impact of trade-in policies

Retail sales accelerated to 5.9% YoY in March, up from 4.0% YoY in the first two months of the year. This is a significant beat from market forecasts for a smaller uptick to 4.3% YoY, and represents the highest level since 2023, a year which benefited from base effects from the pandemic. Over the first quarter, retail sales grew 4.6% YoY.

We are seeing a clear impact from the trade-in policy in the retail sales data. The trade-in policy beneficiary categories, particularly automobiles and household appliances in 2024, were expanded at the start of the year to include more consumer electronics. These include laptops, smartphones, home decoration and renovation materials. Most categories saw growth far eclipsing headline retail sales growth, with household appliances (35.1%) and communication equipment (28.6%) two clear standout categories. Auto sales also managed a respectable 5.5% YoY growth in March.

The "eat, drink, and play" theme continued to see above-average growth, with catering (6.8%), alcohol & tobacco (8.5%), and sports and recreation (26.2%) all seeing solid gains.

The non-beneficiary categories of discretionary consumption generally underperformed. The exceptions were gold and jewelry, which saw growth bounce back to 10.6% YoY.

Trade-in policy is having a clear impact on consumption

Value-added of industry hit highest level since 2021

The value-added of industry also surprised to the upside, accelerating to 7.7% YoY from 5.9% YoY in the first two months of the year. This was the highest rate since the second quarter of 2021.

Manufacturing grew a solid 7.9% YoY on the month, with hi-tech manufacturing, in particular, outperforming at 10.7% YoY. By product output, we saw strong growth in power generation equipment (107.2%), new energy vehicles (40.6%), and industrial robots (16.7%). Semiconductors (9.2%), smartphones (7.0%), and computer equipment (7.8%) saw growth closer to the aggregate level.

Moving forward, the tariff shock is expected to have a spillover effect on manufacturing. Strengthening domestic demand and seeking alternative export destinations will help offset some of the impact. But manufacturing is likely to take a hit, nonetheless. On the brighter side, the US is not a major-end destination for many of the products seeing the fastest growth.

Value added of industry hit highest level since 2021 as hi-tech manufacturing ramps up

Fixed-asset investment edged up as private sector resumed investment

Fixed asset investment (FAI) surprised on the upside, though to a smaller degree compared to retail sales and industrial production. FAI rose 4.2% YoY, year to date, in 1Q25, up from 4.1% in the first two months of the year. One reason was a slight recovery of private investment, which rose to 0.4% YoY ytd. Private sector FAI growth had been flat or in YoY contraction since July 2024. This helped offset public sector FAI slowing to 6.5% YoY from 7.0%.

Manufacturing FAI continued to lead the way, with 9.1% YoY ytd growth. This category continues to benefit from policies to support equipment renewal. It’s also part of a longer-term story of China upgrading its manufacturing infrastructure as it moves up the value-added ladder.

Fixed-asset investment rebounded as private sector FAI growth turned positive

First-quarter data an encouraging start, but more will need to be done to hit this year's growth level

Stronger than expected March data is no doubt a welcome sign for Chinese policymakers, and offers a bit of a buffer for this year's goal to achieve around 5% GDP growth.

Moving forward, the sharp escalation of tariffs will no doubt begin to have a greater impact on activity data. TheApril purchasing managers’ index (PMI), data out a few weeks from now, is likely our first official look at how the impact is unfolding.

The March data shows positive signs that current efforts, namely the trade-in policy and the equipment renewal policy, are having a positive impact on domestic demand.However, assuming US tariffs remain in place for some time and the external demand picture deteriorates more noticeably in the coming months, it's likely that more policy support will be needed.

This expected external drag, combined with continued deflation pressures, presents a strong case for monetary policy easing. Last year's experience showed that bundled stimulus measures tend to have a stronger impact on markets. However, we observe that many voices continue to caution against rate cuts in the interest of currency stabilization. So, it’s possible that a reserve requirement ratio (RRR) cut could come first. We continue to expect 30bp of rate cuts and 100bp of RRR cuts this year.

The upcoming Politburo meeting, expected to take place toward the end of this month, could be a platform for announcing further stimulus measures as well.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: IYH: Healthcare Dashboard For April
data.description:
This monthly article series reports industry metrics, aiming at a top-down analysis of the healthcare sector. It may also help analyze sector ETFs such as Health Care Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLV) and iShares U.S. Healthcare ETF (NYSEARCA:IYH).

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italics describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or unavailable when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively as EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages over a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for healthcare providers in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in this industry.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). In the same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.

Current data

Value and Quality chart

The next chart plots the Value and Quality Scores by industry (higher is better).

Evolution since last month

Due to price action, the value score has improved in the pharmaceuticals/biotechnology and life science tools subsectors.

Momentum

The next chart plots median returns by subsector.

Interpretation

No healthcare subsector is really attractive based on value and quality scores. The life science tools industry is moderately undervalued relative to its historical baseline, but its quality score is quite bad. Healthcare equipment shows good quality metrics, but is slightly overvalued. From a fundamental point of view, the least compelling subsector is pharmaceuticals/biotechnology, with both value and quality scores significantly below the baseline.

Fast facts on IYH

iShares U.S. Healthcare ETF (IYH) was launched on 06/12/2000 and tracks the Russell 1000 Health Care RIC 22.5/45 Capped Index. IYH has 104 holdings and an expense ratio of 0.39%, while XLV charges only 0.09%.

IYH has a concentrated portfolio with significant risks related to the largest companies. Indeed, the top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 56.1% of asset value, and the four largest positions weigh between 6% and 12%.

With a better historical return, lower expense ratio and much higher trading volumes, XLV is more compelling than IYH for long-term investors and traders as well. Nonetheless, XLV is also concentrated, with over 50% of asset value in its top 10 holdings. Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Health Care ETF (RSPH) may be a better choice for investors seeking a healthcare fund with a more balanced portfolio.

Dashboard List

I use the first table to calculate value and quality scores. It may also be used in a stock-picking process to check how companies stand among their peers. For example, the EY column tells us that a large healthcare provider company with an Earnings Yield above 0.0469 (or price/earnings below 21.32) is in the better half of the industry regarding this metric. A Dashboard List is sent every month to Quantitative Risk & Value subscribers with the most profitable companies standing in the better half among their peers regarding the three valuation metrics at the same time. The stocks below are part of the list sent to subscribers a few weeks ago.
